era_context: Late Bronze Age, during the reigns of Ramesses II (Egypt, r. 1279–1213 BCE) and Muwatalli II (Hittites, r. c. 1295–1272 BCE). This was a period of great-power competition in the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, with Egypt and the Hittite Empire as the dominant powers. International trade networks connected the Mediterranean, Near East, and beyond. The Mycenaean Greek world was flourishing. In China, the Shang Dynasty was in its final centuries.

events.title: Egyptian-Hittite diplomatic and military maneuvering
events.description: Egypt and the Hittite Empire continued their competition for control of the Levant, particularly the region around Amurru and Kadesh. Both powers maintained vassal states and conducted military campaigns to assert their authority. This period was characterized by diplomatic correspondence, military preparations, and occasional skirmishes, all leading toward the major confrontation at Kadesh in 1274 BCE.

sources.name: Egyptian temple reliefs and administrative records

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Egyptian-Hittite competition is well-established, but specific events for 1302 BCE are not distinctly documented.

events.title: Mycenaean palatial culture at its height
events.description: The Mycenaean Greek palatial centers (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos, and others) were at the height of their power and prosperity during Late Helladic IIIB. These centers controlled extensive territories and participated in far-reaching trade networks. Linear B script was used for administrative purposes, recording economic transactions, religious offerings, and military organization. The palaces would be destroyed within a century during the Late Bronze Age Collapse.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean sites (pottery, architecture, Linear B tablets from Knossos and Pylos)

events.certainty_note: The archaeological record for Mycenaean Greece in this period is well-established through excavation and stratigraphy.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Mycenaean Late Helladic IIIB remains debated among scholars, with some placing the transition to IIIC earlier or later than traditional chronologies. The significance of individual years within this period is difficult to establish from archaeological evidence alone. Additionally, some scholars question whether the 'palatial culture' was as unified or centralized as traditional narratives suggest.
historiographic_note: For 1302 BCE, the historical record is heavily dependent on Egyptian and Hittite written sources, which emphasize political and military events. Mycenaean Greece is known primarily through archaeology, which provides limited chronological precision for individual years. The absence of contemporary written records from most of the world means that significant events in Africa, Asia, the Americas, and other regions are likely unknown to modern historians. The bias toward literate civilizations distorts our understanding of the period.

graph_edges.note: Escalating Egyptian-Hittite competition led to the Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE
Mycenaean palatial culture of this period was destroyed during the Late Bronze Age Collapse around 1200 BCE
